Gene_Accession_Number.reason: The Results explicitly name Pst wbpL as PSPTO_1755, Pci wbpL candidate as PCH70_40470, and P. aeruginosa PAO1 wbpL as PA3145.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The paper repeatedly refers to WbpL as a glycosyltransferase.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: WbpL is explicitly described as a glycosyltransferase, supporting this family assignment at a general level.

Source_Species.reason: The study directly tests wbpL orthologues in Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato DC3000 and Pseudomonas cichorii ATCC10857/DSM50259.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: These species names are stated throughout the paper for Pst and Pci.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: DC3000 and ATCC10857/DSM50259 are explicitly given for the tested strains.

Applied_Species.reason: The knockout/complementation and phenotype assays were carried out in these two strains.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: These Latin names correspond to the two experimentally used strains named in the Results.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: The varieties/strain designations DC3000 and ATCC10857/DSM50259 are explicitly stated.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The paper directly studies wbpL in lipopolysaccharide O-polysaccharide (OPS) biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The paper states that in P. aeruginosa WbpL is required for initiation of both common polysaccharide antigen (CPA) and O-specific antigen (OSA) synthesis, and discusses orthologous function in OPS initiation.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The Discussion explicitly says WbpL catalyses the initiating step of CPA and OSA biosynthesis in P. aeruginosa; the Results infer the orthologues likely also initiate OPS synthesis.

Primary_Product.reason: No specific product such as a 'lipid-linked initial OPS repeat precursor' is named or characterized in this paper.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Supported in substance: the paper states WbpL is a glycosyltransferase required for initiation of OPS synthesis and, in P. aeruginosa, initiates both CPA and OSA biosynthesis.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: Deletion of wbpL caused loss of OPS in Pst and Pci, showing it is required for synthesis of the terminal product measured.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper supports WbpL as an essential/key initiating enzyme, but does not directly test or state that it is rate-limiting.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The terminal product directly assessed is the O-polysaccharide (OPS) portion of lipopolysaccharide.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: OPS is explicitly an O-polysaccharide, i.e. a polysaccharide.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Directly inferable from the paper because OPS is analyzed as part of LPS, a major outer membrane component of Gram-negative bacteria.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Results show OPS presence is associated with efficient host colonization, swarming motility, and full virulence in planta.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: All listed phenotypes are supported by the Results: knockout caused OPS-deficient LPS, reduced swarming, reduced entry after spray inoculation, reduced apoplastic amplification in Pst, and reduced disease symptoms/virulence.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: These methods are all described and used in the Results: targeted knockout, complementation/cross-complementation, LPS extraction with SDS-PAGE/NuPAGE and silver staining, GPC, infection assays, and swarming assays.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: This summary accurately reflects the Results and Discussion without introducing unsupported specifics.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The paper does not report or test WbpL subcellular localization, nor explicitly call it inner-membrane or membrane-associated in this study.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: These experimental conditions are explicitly described in the infection and swarming assay sections.

Applied_Promoters.reason: The complementation constructs are explicitly said to place the gene under control of the promoter from the GmR cassette.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: WbpK and WbpM are discussed as neighboring/conserved OPS-related proteins, but direct physical or functional interaction with WbpL was not experimentally shown in this paper.

Omics_Data.reason: The study includes comparative genomics via BLASTP and synteny analysis; no accession-specific omics dataset is reported.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Supported by Results/Discussion: loss of OPS reduced motility and virulence, and complementation in Pci was only partial with altered OPS substitution frequency.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is a plausible application inferred from the virulence data, but the paper does not explicitly propose breeding/engineering or antimicrobial targeting as an application.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The Results explicitly show lack of Pst self-complementation, suggest dosage/context effects, and show that Pst-wbpL complements the Pci mutant, supporting conserved function.

corrections.reason: No specific product such as a 'lipid-linked initial OPS repeat precursor' is named or characterized in this paper.

corrections.old_value: cytoplasmic membrane-associated inner-membrane glycosyltransferase, inferred from role in O-antigen initiation
corrections.reason: The paper does not report or test WbpL subcellular localization, nor explicitly call it inner-membrane or membrane-associated in this study.
